summary:
Dante Alighieri's Divine Comedy (La Divina Commedia, composed c. 1308–1320 AD) is the supreme literary achievement of the medieval world and one of the greatest works in the history of world literature — an allegorical narrative poem in three parts (Inferno, Purgatorio, Paradiso) describing the poet's journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven guided by Virgil and then Beatrice, encompassing a comprehensive vision of medieval Christian cosmology, ethics, history, and politics. Written in Tuscan Italian rather than Latin, the Comedy was instrumental in establishing the Tuscan dialect as the literary standard of the Italian language.

The Inferno — the most widely read section — depicts Hell as a series of nine concentric circles where sinners suffer punishments precisely fitted to their earthly transgressions (contrapasso), peopled with historical and mythological figures including Dante's political enemies, who appear in Hell, and his heroes and mentors, who appear in Purgatory and Paradise. The poem's topical political commentary — placing living popes in Hell, celebrating the Ghibelline political cause — reflected Dante's bitter exile from Florence in 1302 following a political reversal; he never returned to the city that had banished him, dying in Ravenna in 1321. The Divine Comedy synthesized the scholastic theology of [[event:2293|Thomas Aquinas]], the political philosophy of Aristotle, the classical learning of Virgil and Ovid, and the mystical theology of Bernard of Clairvaux into a work of such density, beauty, and ambition that T.S. Eliot declared Dante and Shakespeare the two poets who 'divide the modern world between them.'
